claim: Frozen test files were modified during implementation: frozen tests changed: tests/main/orky-registry-service.test.ts (modified), tests/main/orky-root-engine.test.ts (modified)
fix: Revert the test changes. Tests are frozen after phase 4. If a test is genuinely wrong, route the issue back to phase 4 (Tests) instead of editing tests during implementation.

location: src/main/ipc/register-registry.ts:22-25 (all four ipcMain.handle registrations) + src/main/ipc/register.ts:77 (registerRegistry(orkyRegistry, send) call site, no sender predicate passed) + .orky/features/0005-cross-project-orky-registry/03-plan.md TASK-011
claim: REQ-016 (FROZEN spec) explicitly states: 'The new handlers MUST also follow the existing per-window sender discipline of register-orky.ts where applicable (an app-global service still validates the sender per CLAUDE.md's read-only/sender-scoped pattern).' register-orky.ts's onWatch/onUnwatch both gate on `isKnownWindowSender(e.sender)` before doing anything (the FINDING-SEC-002-era hardening, now widened per the new CLAUDE.md 'Orky registry' gotcha). register-registry.ts has NO equivalent check on any of its four `ipcMain.handle` registrations (registry:current, registry:roots, registry:addRoot, registry:removeRoot) — `(_e, root: unknown) => registry.addRoot(root)` ignores `_e` (the event/sender) entirely. The composition root confirms this is not merely an oversight in the registrar file: `registerRegistry(orkyRegistry, send)` in register.ts:77 is never even given `wm.isKnownWindowSender` to call (compare `registerOrky(send, (sender) => wm.isKnownWindowSender(sender), onPaneRoot, orkyEngine)` on the line above, which IS given it). Tracing to root cause: 03-plan.md TASK-011 (tagged 'Satisfies: REQ-016') prescribes the exact `ipcMain.handle(CH.registryAddRoot, (_e, root: unknown) => registry.addRoot(root))` pattern with no sender-check step, so the plan itself dropped the obligation the spec stated, and implementation faithfully followed the (incomplete) plan. Practical impact today is bounded (contextIsolation:true, nodeIntegration:false, no webviewTag, and every current BrowserWindow is created via WindowManager and thus IS a 'known' sender), but the two mutating handlers (registry:addRoot/removeRoot) write to disk (orky-registry.json) and begin tracking arbitrary filesystem roots with zero defense-in-depth against a foreign/destroyed/untracked webContents reaching the channel — exactly the class of regression the codebase's own established pattern (and this spec's explicit MUST) exists to prevent, and a meaningfully bigger blast radius than the read-only orky:watch/unwatch channels that DO have the check.
fix: Thread the same sender predicate registerOrky already receives into registerRegistry: change its signature to `registerRegistry(registry: OrkyRegistry, send: Send, isKnownWindowSender: (sender: WebContents) => boolean): Disposer` and gate every handler, e.g. `ipcMain.handle(CH.registryAddRoot, (e, root: unknown) => isKnownWindowSender(e.sender) ? registry.addRoot(root) : { ok: false, roots: registry.roots(), error: 'unknown sender' })` (mirror for removeRoot; current/roots can simply return the current snapshot/roots — or reject — for an unknown sender, whichever the team prefers, but they must not silently skip the check). Update the call site to `registerRegistry(orkyRegistry, send, (sender) => wm.isKnownWindowSender(sender))`. Add a test mirroring register-orky.test's foreign-sender coverage.
resolution: Verified fixed in this loopback iteration. src/main/ipc/register-registry.ts now threads `isKnownWindowSender: (sender: WebContents) => boolean = () => true` as a 3rd constructor-style param and gates all four ipcMain.handle registrations on it (registry:current/registry:roots return [] for an unknown sender rather than leaking the aggregate/list; registry:addRoot/registry:removeRoot return a RegistryMutationResult-shaped rejection). The permissive default exists only so the FROZEN tests/main/register-registry.test.ts (which invokes handlers with a fake {} event lacking a real .sender) keep passing unmodified -- confirmed the REAL production call site, src/main/ipc/register.ts:77, explicitly passes the real predicate `(sender) => wm.isKnownWindowSender(sender)` (mirroring the registerOrky call immediately above it on line 76), and grep confirms register.ts is the ONLY production call site of registerRegistry -- so the default value never takes effect in production; the gap is genuinely closed. Independent adversarial review of this same loopback's diff did surface a related-but-distinct issue in the rejection payload itself (the addRoot/removeRoot unknown-sender branches return the REAL registry.roots() instead of [] in the `roots` field, contradicting this file's own stated never-leak invariant for current/roots) -- filed separately as FINDING-SEC-009 rather than reopening this finding, since the core REQ-016 gap (total absence of sender validation) this finding identified is fixed.

claim: orky:watch/orky:unwatch validate the WINDOW sender (isKnownWindowSender) and that `id`/`cwd` are strings, but never validate that `id` is an actual, sender-owned pane (no claimPane-style ownership check, unlike register-pty.ts's claimPane/paneOwner discipline). This gap pre-dates 0005 (present in 0004 too), but its blast radius was previously a single pane-scoped `orky:status` push routed back to the SAME window that sent it. Feature 0005 wires the identical unvalidated (id, cwd) pair into the new `onPaneRoot` hook -> `OrkyRegistry.trackPaneRoot(id, root)` (register.ts:69, registerOrky's new 4th param), which now feeds the APP-GLOBAL, all-windows-broadcast `registry:status` aggregate (REQ-002/REQ-020) and is queryable via `registry:current`. Concretely: any renderer code path reaching `window.api.orkyWatch` can pass a fabricated paneId (not tied to any real PTY) together with an arbitrary local `cwd` string, causing the engine to open a chokidar watcher and roll up status for whatever `.orky/`-containing directory that cwd resolves to, and inject a phantom `source:'pane'` entry into the cross-project registry visible to every window — persisting indefinitely (and leaking a watcher/timer) unless a matching `orky:unwatch(id)` is later sent for that same fabricated id, since nothing tears it down via real pane-close lifecycle. This is a widening of a pre-existing gap's consequences, not a brand-new vulnerability class, and pane ids are unguessable UUIDs (src/renderer/store.ts uses `uuid` via workspace-model.ts's injected IdGen) so collision with a REAL pane's id to hijack its state is not practical — the realistic risk is phantom-root injection / registry pollution / a watcher-leak DoS, not pane takeover.
fix: Thread real pane ownership through the same path register-pty.ts already established for claimPane: expose a `wm.isPaneOwnedBy(id, sender): boolean` (or reuse `claimPane`'s `paneOwner` map) and require it in register-orky.ts's onWatch/onUnwatch before calling `orky.watch`/`onPaneRoot`, OR (narrower, 0005-scoped fix) have `OrkyRegistry.trackPaneRoot` only accept a paneId that is verifiably a live, currently-tracked pane (e.g. cross-check against the renderer's pane registry/workspace store rather than trusting any string the IPC layer hands it). At minimum, document the accepted risk explicitly if left as-is.

claim: OrkyRegistryEntry carries a `[key: string]: unknown` index signature whose own doc comment admits it exists so 'test code can structurally cast a snapshot to Record<string, unknown>[]'. Verified independently (scratch tsc repro): removing the index signature makes tests/main/orky-registry-service.test.ts:120's `(registry.current() as Array<Record<string, unknown>>)[0]` fail to compile with TS2352 ('neither type sufficiently overlaps with the other') -- the index signature was added to a shared, wire-level production type (also the IPC contract's `registry:current`/`registry:status` payload shape, which the future F6 renderer will consume) purely to satisfy one test's loose cast, rather than the test doing its own narrowing (`as unknown as Record<string, unknown>[]`). The index signature silently disables TypeScript's excess-property checking and typo detection for every other consumer of OrkyRegistryEntry/OrkyRegistrySnapshot going forward.
fix: Remove the `[key: string]: unknown` index signature from OrkyRegistryEntry in src/shared/types.ts. Fix the test with an explicit double assertion (`registry.current() as unknown as Array<Record<string, unknown>>`), matching the idiom already used elsewhere in the suite for structural-shape assertions.

location: src/main/orky/orky-registry.ts:65-73 (init, now exclusive()-wrapped), 97-133 (addRoot/removeRoot), 144-148 (dispose -- still NOT exclusive()-wrapped), 170-174 (exclusive)
claim:
ORIGINAL CLAIM: OrkyRegistry.init() is NOT routed through the `exclusive()` mutation-serialization queue that addRoot()/removeRoot() use, even though 03-plan.md TASK-007 explicitly groups all three together ('Session-identity race-pattern reuse: addRoot/removeRoot/init each await ...; guard against a concurrent add/remove/dispose superseding mid-flight exactly like UsageTracker (REQ-019)') and REQ-019 requires the session-identity race pattern for 'any per-root async work that awaits between claiming a slot and using it.' init()'s for-loop awaits `engine.addConsumer` once per persisted root without claiming or re-checking any identity token, and its `this.persistedRoots = await this.store.load()` assignment is a bare, un-queued field write that can race with a concurrently-queued addRoot()/removeRoot() called before init() resolves (last-write-wins data loss on `persistedRoots`), and a concurrent dispose() mid-init can leave init()'s later `addConsumer` calls registering watchers on the shared engine AFTER this registry instance has already unsubscribed/cleared its listeners -- an orphaned watcher dispose() can never close, exactly the leak REQ-019's acceptance criterion ('after dispose(), the service holds zero open watchers') exists to rule out.

RE-VERIFIED this loopback (quality-lens re-review, partial fix): `init()` is now wrapped in `return this.exclusive(async () => {...})` (src/main/orky/orky-registry.ts:65-73). This DOES close the half of the race that caused actual data corruption: a concurrently-queued `addRoot()`/`removeRoot()` can no longer interleave with init()'s `this.persistedRoots = await this.store.load()` read-modify-write -- both now run through the SAME `mutationChain`, so the 'last-write-wins data loss on persistedRoots' scenario is genuinely closed. Confirmed no new deadlock/double-queue risk: `exclusive()` (lines 170-174) is a plain promise-chain (`mutationChain.then(fn, fn)`), not a re-entrant lock with acquire/release semantics, so a hypothetical second `init()` call would simply queue behind the first and re-run harmlessly (`engine.addConsumer` is idempotent per `(consumerId, orkyDir)` -- src/main/orky/orky-root-engine.ts:86-89); production has exactly one `init()` call site (src/main/ipc/register.ts:70), awaited once.

HOWEVER the OTHER half of the SAME originally-described race is NOT closed: `dispose()` (lines 144-148) remains a plain synchronous method that is never routed through `exclusive()`, so it is never serialized against an in-flight `init()`. Traced the actual consequence: if `dispose()` fires while `init()`'s for-loop is suspended between `addConsumer` calls, `init()`'s later iterations still call `engine.addConsumer('persisted:<root>', ...)` for roots `dispose()` hadn't yet processed -- and `OrkyRootEngine.addConsumer` has no 'am I disposed' flag, so it will happily open a brand-new chokidar watcher on an engine instance the registry already considers torn down (the engine's own session-identity re-check only protects an `addConsumer` call that was ALREADY in-flight at the moment of dispose, not a later iteration of the SAME for-loop that hasn't called `addConsumer` yet). This is a genuine, still-open violation of REQ-019's acceptance criterion ('after dispose(), the service holds zero open watchers'), and the new `init()` doc comment added by this loopback's own diff overstates the fix ('a concurrent addRoot/removeRoot/dispose racing against an in-flight init() can never interleave' -- dispose() is precisely the case where that claim does not hold). In production this remains unreachable ONLY because src/main/ipc/register.ts:70 awaits `orkyRegistry.init()` to completion before `wm.onAllWindowsClosed` (the only call path to `dispose()`) is even wired up -- the EXACT SAME external sequencing invariant the original finding already identified as the sole reason production is safe today, and it is still undocumented as a class-level precondition. No test exercises a concurrent `dispose()` during an in-flight `init()` (every test in tests/main/orky-registry-service.test.ts, e.g. TEST-129, awaits `init()` to completion before calling `dispose()`), so this narrower remaining gap is untested as well as unfixed.
fix: Either (a) also route `dispose()` through the SAME `exclusive()` queue (note: this changes `dispose()`'s public signature from sync `void` to `Promise<void>`, which ripples to every call site, including the composition root's combined `() => { orkyRegistry.dispose(); orkyEngine.dispose() }` disposer in register.ts and any other synchronous teardown ordering that assumes dispose() completes synchronously), or (b) cheaper: give `OrkyRegistry` a `disposed` boolean set synchronously at the very top of `dispose()`, and have `init()`'s for-loop (and `trackPaneRoot`/`addRoot`'s `addConsumer` calls, for symmetry) check it after every `await` and bail out before calling `engine.addConsumer` if set. Either way, correct the `init()` doc comment so it no longer claims `dispose()` is covered until one of these lands. Add a regression test mirroring TEST-129 but exercising the CONCURRENT case: start `init()` against a deferred/slow `store.load()` or `addConsumer`, call `dispose()` while it is still in-flight, and assert the engine ends up with zero open watchers/consumers for every persisted root (not just the ones dispose() happened to see at the moment it ran).
resolution: Verified fixed in this loopback iteration (quality-lens re-review, option (b) from this finding's own fix text). Re-read src/main/orky/orky-registry.ts:31-204 in full. A new `private disposed = false` field exists (line 39). `dispose()` (lines 162-167) sets `this.disposed = true` as its literal FIRST statement, before removing any consumer, before `unsubscribeEngine()`, before clearing listeners -- so the flag is visible to any code that runs after dispose() returns, including a suspended init(). `init()` (still wrapped in `exclusive()`, lines 73-84) checks `if (this.disposed) return` at THREE points: immediately on entry (line 75, before the first await), immediately after `await this.store.load()` (line 77), and immediately after EVERY iteration's `await this.engine.addConsumer(...)` inside the for-loop (line 80, inside the loop body, directly after the await) -- i.e. after every single await point in the method, not a subset. Traced the race exhaustively: JS is single-threaded and `dispose()` itself contains no `await`, so once invoked it runs to completion atomically in one microtask/tick -- the only moments it can interleave with init() are the gaps where init() is suspended awaiting a promise (store.load(), or one of the per-root addConsumer calls), or the gap between init() being called and its queued exclusive() callback actually starting (also a suspension point, closed by the entry check). Every one of those gaps is immediately followed by a disposed check before the next side-effecting statement (the loop's own recompute() after the last check has no intervening await, so it cannot be reached in a torn state). Therefore init() can never call engine.addConsumer after a dispose() has already run -- the exact 'orphaned watcher dispose() can never close' scenario this finding described is now closed, mirroring the CLAUDE.md session-identity race pattern (claim/flag checked after every await) rather than routing dispose() through exclusive() (which was correctly rejected, per this finding's own fix text (a), because it would make dispose() asynchronous and break TEST-129's synchronous `registry.dispose(); expect(...)` assertion with no await). Confirmed no new problem: `disposed` is read only inside this class (grepped the whole src/main/orky/ tree and register.ts/services.ts -- no external reader); dispose() remains safe to call twice (idempotent) -- re-setting `disposed = true` is a no-op, re-iterating `persistedRoots` calling `engine.removeConsumer` on already-removed ids is a documented silent no-op (OrkyRootEngine.detach: `if (!sess) return`), `unsubscribeEngine()` a second time is a harmless `Set.delete` on an already-absent callback, and `listeners.clear()` on an empty Set is a no-op; production has exactly one dispose() call site (src/main/ipc/register.ts:83, wired to window-close teardown) so double-dispose is untested but verified safe by inspection. Noted, not blocking: addRoot()/removeRoot()/trackPaneRoot() were NOT given symmetric disposed checks (the fix text's option (b) mentioned this 'for symmetry' but the finding's core claim was specifically about init() vs dispose()) -- this is a pre-existing, narrower gap outside this finding's scope, not introduced or worsened by this fix, and out of scope for FINDING-QUAL-002 specifically. No new regression test exercising a concurrent dispose()-during-in-flight-init() was added (the fix text's own suggested addition), matching the same documented tradeoff already accepted for this loopback's FINDING-SEC-009/FINDING-SEC-010 resolutions in this same file -- the fix itself was verified correct by direct code inspection and exhaustive reasoning about JS's single-threaded await-gap semantics rather than by a new fault-injection test. `npm run build` passes clean. `npm test` passes 845/845 (127/127 files), including TEST-129, run with TEMP/TMP redirected to a scratch directory OUTSIDE the contaminated OS temp tree (the real user Temp dir contains a stray `.orky` with audit.json/config.json that findOrkyRoot's bounded ancestor walk picks up if the scratch TEMP override is merely a subdirectory NESTED under that same contaminated tree -- confirmed this by reproducing the failure with a nested override and fixing it by pointing TEMP/TMP at a directory outside that tree entirely, e.g. a top-level drive path; the real contaminated dir was never touched).

location: src/main/orky/orky-registry.ts:42,69,89,105 (line numbers shifted from the original 42,60,78,94 -- this loopback's FINDING-DA-001/FINDING-SEC-010 fixes inserted the new `resolveCanonical` method above them; same 4 call sites, same shape)
claim:
The `<root>/.orky` path is reconstructed via the literal `join(root, '.orky')` at three separate call sites in OrkyRegistry (init, trackPaneRoot, addRoot), and the engine's `onStatus` subscriber inverts it back via `dirname(orkyDir)` at line 42 -- the root<->orkyDir relationship is duplicated as an implicit string-path convention in two directions across four call sites instead of being expressed once. Low risk today since the convention is simple and stable, but it is exactly the kind of small, scattered duplication that drifts silently if the root<->orkyDir mapping ever changes.

RE-VERIFIED this loopback (out of scope, not fixed, accuracy check only): still matches current code byte-for-byte in substance -- the same 3 forward `join(<var>, '.orky')` call sites (init, trackPaneRoot, addRoot) plus the 1 inverse `dirname(orkyDir)` call (constructor's onStatus subscriber) exist; only their line numbers drifted because this loopback's DA-001/SEC-010 fixes inserted the new `resolveCanonical` private method (and its own call sites at `trackPaneRoot`/`addRoot`/`removeRoot`) above them. `resolveCanonical` itself does NOT add, remove, or alter any `join(..., '.orky')` call site -- it compares already-`resolve()`d root strings via `normalizeProjectRoot` (pure case/slash folding, no `.orky` suffix involved) -- so the call-site count/shape this finding describes is materially unchanged. Location field updated above for the line-number drift only.
fix: Extract a small `orkyDirOf(root: string): string` (and its inverse, e.g. `projectRootOf(orkyDir: string): string` wrapping `dirname`) and use it at all four call sites so the relationship is single-sourced.

location: src/main/orky/orky-registry.ts:91-103 (trackPaneRoot) + :175-184 (resolveCanonical, returns first-seen spelling verbatim) + :42-43 (constructor keys statusByRoot by dirname(orkyDir)) / REQ-002, REQ-006
claim: REQ-002 mandates membership be 'keyed and de-duplicated by RESOLVED project-root absolute path' and REQ-006 that each entry's `root` be 'the resolved project-root absolute path ... normalized'. LOOPBACK RE-VERIFY (this iteration's resolveCanonical fix): the DEDUP symptom this finding originally led with IS now masked -- empirically confirmed on this win32 box (throwaway node repro, discarded) that a case/slash-divergent pair collapses to EXACTLY ONE `source:'both'` entry in BOTH arrival orderings (addRoot-first and pane-first), because addRoot/trackPaneRoot both run their input through resolveCanonical, which folds via normalizeProjectRoot (path.resolve + win32 toLowerCase) and reuses the first-seen spelling. HOWEVER the ROOT CAUSE this finding named -- 'the pane side stores findOrkyRoot()'s RAW return verbatim and is NEVER resolve()'d' -- is NOT closed: resolveCanonical returns its `root` argument UNCHANGED (case- AND slash-preserved, never resolve()'d) whenever it is the FIRST-seen spelling of a root (orky-registry.ts:183 `return root`). So a project first observed via a forward-slash-with-drive-letter pane cwd (reachable on Windows via an OSC-9;9 emitter reporting a forward-slash path, the cwd-parser.ts:17 non-DOS OSC-7 fallback, or cygpath -m style output -- NOTE the standard OSC-7 DOS path IS backslash-converted at cwd-parser.ts:12, so this is narrower than the finding originally implied but still genuinely reachable) is stored in paneRoots and in the snapshot with its RAW membership key, e.g. 'c:/dev/proj', while the constructor's onStatus subscriber keys statusByRoot by dirname(join(root,'.orky')) = 'c:\dev\proj' (path.join normalizes to backslash on win32). The two keys NEVER match, so buildRegistrySnapshot's `statusByRoot.get(root) ?? null` yields status:null -- the aggregate entry surfaces status:null EVEN THOUGH the engine successfully read the roll-up, AND even after the entry collapses to source:'both'. Empirically reproduced: pane-first fwd-slash scenario -> { root:'c:/dev/proj', source:'both', status:null }; the pane-ONLY non-dedup case (no persisted root at all) -> { root:'c:/dev/proj', source:'pane', status:null }; whereas addRoot-first (backslash) -> status correctly attaches. This silently defeats the whole point of the cross-project aggregate (the F6 queue / F9 pane / F13 notifications substrate) for that project -- it is tracked but perpetually blank. Two further consequences of the same un-normalized spelling: (1) because resolveCanonical PREFERS the already-seen pane spelling, addRoot can WRITE that forward-slash spelling into the DURABLE persisted list (REQ-012, F7's write-allowlist source) and OrkyRegistryStore treats roots as OPAQUE strings (no path.resolve on load/save -- orky-registry-store.ts:8-17), so the bad spelling persists across restarts and never self-heals; (2) the entry's `root` field is then 'c:/dev/proj', not the REQ-006-mandated 'resolved ... normalized' absolute path. The frozen suite cannot catch this (see FINDING-DA-002): it only ever injects pre-canonicalized, byte-identical mkdtemp strings into both sources, so both the status-null and the non-normalized-persist cases are structurally unreachable in the tests.
fix: Canonicalize the pane root to the RESOLVED, join-consistent form AT the point it becomes a key -- in trackPaneRoot, store path.resolve(root) (NOT the raw string) when resolveCanonical finds no existing match, so paneRoots/persistedRoots keys agree byte-for-byte with the engine's dirname(join(root,'.orky')) statusByRoot key. Equivalently, single-source the membership<->status join: key statusByRoot the SAME way membership is keyed, or route validate-root, the store's load/save, addRoot/removeRoot, trackPaneRoot AND the constructor's statusByRoot.set through ONE shared resolve()-based normalizer so all four derive a byte-identical key for the same physical directory. Then extend the FINDING-DA-002 integration test to also assert the collapsed entry carries the REAL (non-null) status, not merely source:'both' -- and add a pane-only forward-slash case asserting status is non-null. propose CONV: every key used to identify the same filesystem path across data structures (dedup/membership key, status key, watcher/consumer id) MUST be derived from ONE shared path.resolve()-based normalizer, so no data structure keys a physical directory by a raw or differently-normalized spelling than another -- never mix a source-dependent raw string for one key with a join()/dirname()-normalized string for another.
resolution: Verified RESOLVED this loopback (devils-advocate re-review of the FINDING-DA-001 residual gap). resolveCanonical (src/main/orky/orky-registry.ts:183-192) now returns `resolve(root)` on the no-existing-match path (line 191), NOT the raw `root` verbatim. Independently traced by hand AND empirically reproduced on this win32 box with a throwaway node script OUTSIDE the repo (created under C:\ttmp-da001v2-script, since deleted). Confirmed: (1) PANE-FIRST FORWARD-SLASH (the exact status-null repro): trackPaneRoot('p','c:/dev/proj') with no existing entries -> resolveCanonical returns resolve('c:/dev/proj') = 'c:\dev\proj' (slash-canonical, drive-case preserved), stored in paneRoots; the engine's onStatus keys statusByRoot by dirname(join('c:\dev\proj','.orky')) = 'c:\dev\proj', BYTE-IDENTICAL to the membership key, so buildRegistrySnapshot's statusByRoot.get(root) now HITS and status attaches (non-null) instead of coming back null -- the finding's precise reproduction is closed. (2) DEDUP holds in BOTH orderings: addRoot-first-then-pane AND pane-first-then-addRoot (case+slash-divergent) each collapse onto EXACTLY ONE canonical entry (persistedRoots[0] === the paneRoots value in both), via normalizeProjectRoot's case/slash fold reusing the first-seen, now-resolve()d spelling. (3) REQ-006 PERSISTED VALUE: what OrkyRegistryStore.save writes is slash-canonical (contains no forward slash) in BOTH orderings -- addRoot stores resolveCanonical(v.root), which is resolve(v.root) on no-match and an already-resolved existing spelling on match, so a forward-slash spelling can no longer reach the durable list; consequences (1)/(2) of the original claim (bad spelling persisted across restarts / entry.root non-normalized) are closed. (4) NEW-GAP CHECK: resolve('C:Dev') (drive-relative) resolves against cwd -- surprising but NOT newly reachable, since pane roots come from findOrkyRoot's ancestor walk (always absolute) and addRoot already ran resolve() upstream in validateRegistryRoot; the fix only makes the no-match value MORE canonical, never less. removeRoot's resolveCanonical(resolve(input)) returns resolve(resolve(input)) = resolve(input) on no-match (identical to pre-fix, which already fed resolve(input)) and the existing canonical spelling on match (the already-resolved FINDING-SEC-010 behavior) -- no surprising effect. No remaining CODE-DRIVEN path stores a raw/un-resolved value: trackPaneRoot and addRoot both route through resolveCanonical, removeRoot resolves, and the store now only ever receives resolve()d values. OUT OF SCOPE, noted (does NOT reopen this finding): OrkyRegistryStore.load()/normalizeRoots treat roots as OPAQUE (no resolve on load) and init() loads them verbatim into persistedRoots, so a HAND-EDITED orky-registry.json carrying a forward-slash entry would still key membership by that raw spelling and mismatch the backslash statusByRoot key (status:null). That is (a) unreachable via any app-driven flow now that every addRoot write is resolve()d, and (b) not legacy-reachable for this not-yet-released feature (empty store on first run) -- a distinct, narrower hand-edit-only robustness gap, not DA-001's status-null-through-normal-use defect, which IS genuinely closed. npm run build passes; npm test 845/845 (127/127 files), run with TEMP/TMP redirected to C:\ttmp-da001v2 outside the contaminated OS temp tree (both scratch dirs removed afterward; the real contaminated dir was never touched).

claim: The registry integration suite injects pane membership by calling registry.trackPaneRoot(id, canonicalPath) directly with mkdtemp-canonical absolute paths, and feeds addRoot the SAME canonical strings -- it never drives the real OrkyTracker.watch -> findOrkyRoot -> onPaneRoot -> trackPaneRoot chain from a raw OSC-reported cwd. So the central REQ-002 invariant (de-dup by RESOLVED root; pane+persisted collapse to source:'both'; no duplicate entry) is only ever exercised where pane-string === persisted-string by construction. The suite is structurally incapable of catching FINDING-DA-001 (pane side not resolve()'d; case/slash divergence on Windows): the gate proves a test exists for REQ-002, not a test that exercises its hard case (two source-divergent spellings of one physical root). REQ-002's de-dup acceptance is therefore unverified for any realistic cwd that is not already byte-identical to the persisted form.
fix: Add an integration test that resolves a pane root through the REAL findOrkyRoot/OrkyTracker.watch path from a non-canonical cwd (a forward-slash and/or differing-case spelling of an addRoot'd root) and asserts a SINGLE source:'both' entry -- the genuine REQ-002 collapse case the current TEST-112 fakes away.

claim: The spec never decides what an entry's status carries for a project that is present and readable but wholly IDLE (no active.json; features parked before the 'active' phase). Because REQ-005 reuses orky-status.ts verbatim and that mapper deliberately EXCLUDES idle features from OrkyPaneStatus.features (frozen 0004 invariant TEST-018), such a project surfaces status.kind:'idle' with features:[] -- i.e. the cross-project aggregate (the explicit substrate for F6's decision queue, F9's native pane, and F13's OS notifications per 00-intake.md) carries ZERO feature detail for it. Whether that is intended was never specified; it was settled de-facto during implementation when TEST-106/TEST-127 (which originally asserted idle features ARE surfaced) had to be re-pointed at kind:'idle'/features:[]. This is a missing-requirement: a cross-cutting product-semantics decision for every downstream T1 consumer was made by implementation convenience (verbatim mapper reuse) rather than spec deliberation, so F6/F9/F13 inherit an unstated contract.
fix: Add a REQ (or an explicit note under REQ-006) stating whether the aggregate intentionally surfaces no per-feature detail for genuinely-idle (non-stalled, non-active, non-awaiting-human) tracked projects, and confirm that is acceptable for the F6 queue / F9 pane / F13 notifications -- or define how an idle-but-tracked project should be represented. Record the decision so downstream features build on a stated contract.

claim: trackPaneRoot unconditionally does removeConsumer('pane:<id>') then (voided) addConsumer('pane:<id>',...) whenever the pane already had a prior root, with NO early-out when the new root equals the prior root. The renderer (OrkyWatcher.tsx:16) re-issues orky:watch on every cwd-STRING change, so any cd WITHIN a project re-resolves to the SAME root and fires trackPaneRoot(id, sameRoot). For a root whose only consumer is that one pane (not persisted, no sibling pane), the registry's removeConsumer tears the shared chokidar watcher down and the voided addConsumer immediately re-opens it plus forces a fresh full reread -- ON TOP OF the identical close/open OrkyTracker.watch already performed for the same event (the 'pane:<id>' consumer id is SHARED between the tracker and the registry, double-owned). That roughly DOUBLES 0004's per-cd watcher churn and adds an extra reread, which REQ-014's 'This MUST NOT regress 0004's single-root cost' exists to prevent (chokidar teardown/recreate is expensive on Windows / ReadDirectoryChangesW). Steady-state watcher COUNT stays 1, so the literal one-watcher acceptance passes while the per-event cost silently regresses.
fix: Early-return in trackPaneRoot when the resolved root is unchanged (`if (prev === root) return`), and/or stop having OrkyRegistry independently manage the 'pane:<id>' consumer OrkyTracker already owns -- derive pane membership from a registry-private consumer namespace (e.g. 'reg-pane:<id>') or from engine.getConsumers, so two owners never double-churn one shared consumer id.

claim: REQ-015 bounds the PER-ROOT read cost (MAX_FEATURE_DIRS, MAX_FILE_BYTES, both warn-on-cap) but nothing bounds the NUMBER of tracked roots. Unlike open-pane roots (naturally bounded by live panes), the persisted list is user-controlled AND durable across restarts: addRoot has no cap, the store's normalizeRoots applies no count limit, and init() opens one chokidar watcher per persisted root with a full-set snapshot rebuilt + broadcast to ALL windows (REQ-008) on every change of any of them. A hand-edited or slowly-accumulated orky-registry.json with hundreds/thousands of roots spawns that many watchers at startup and turns every state.json write anywhere into an O(N) snapshot broadcast. The spec dismissed large-N as 'low cardinality' (resolved-open-question #3) without bounding the one membership dimension that is genuinely unbounded and persistent.
fix: Cap the persisted root count: reject addRoot past a stated limit with a CONV-001 actionable error, and cap+warn on load (mirroring the per-root MAX_* warn-on-cap discipline), so the unbounded dimension is bounded the same way the per-root dimensions already are.

location: src/main/ipc/register-registry.ts:47-58 (registry:addRoot / registry:removeRoot unknown-sender rejection branches) vs :39-46 (registry:current / registry:roots unknown-sender branches) — this loopback's FINDING-SEC-007 fix
claim: The FINDING-SEC-007 fix (now verified resolved) gates all four registry:* handlers on isKnownWindowSender, and the file's own doc comment states the explicit design goal: 'the two pure reads (current/roots) return an empty result ([]) rather than leaking the real aggregate/list to an unrecognized sender' — and registry:current/registry:roots do exactly that (`return []`). But registry:addRoot and registry:removeRoot's unknown-sender branches do NOT follow that same rule: both return `{ ok: false, roots: registry.roots(), error: 'unknown sender' }` — i.e. they populate the rejection's `roots` field with the REAL, current persisted root list (absolute filesystem paths, which on Windows routinely embed the OS username via `C:\Users\<name>\...`), not `[]`. This is a self-contradiction within the same file/feature: the exact unrecognized-sender threat model the SEC-007 fix exists to defend against (a foreign/destroyed/un-registered webContents reaching a registry:* channel) is precisely the scenario in which this rejection payload would fire, and it leaks the persisted-list data the current/roots branches were deliberately written to withhold. RegistryMutationResult's `roots` field is non-optional, which likely explains why the author reached for `registry.roots()` (a value already in scope) rather than `[]`, but the effect is an inconsistent, partially-defeated version of the same-file's own stated no-leak invariant. As with the parent SEC-007 finding, practical reachability today is bounded (contextIsolation:true, nodeIntegration:false, every BrowserWindow created via WindowManager, so isKnownWindowSender is true for every real renderer in normal operation) — this is a defense-in-depth gap, not an actively exploited one, but it is a concrete, demonstrable inconsistency in code that exists specifically to provide that defense-in-depth.
fix: Change both unknown-sender rejection branches in registry:addRoot/registry:removeRoot to `return { ok: false, roots: [], error: 'unknown sender' }`, matching registry:current/registry:roots' established no-leak precedent in the same file. Add a test (the foreign-sender coverage FINDING-SEC-007's own fix text already called for) asserting the addRoot/removeRoot rejection payload for an unrecognized sender never contains a real persisted-root entry.
resolution: Verified fixed in this loopback iteration. Re-read src/main/ipc/register-registry.ts:49-60: both the registry:addRoot and registry:removeRoot unknown-sender rejection branches now return `{ ok: false, roots: [], error: 'unknown sender' }` -- a literal empty array, not `registry.roots()` -- matching the no-leak precedent already established by the registry:current/registry:roots branches immediately above (which return `[]` for an unrecognized sender). The file's own header comment was updated in the same diff to state this explicitly, citing FINDING-SEC-009 by name ('deliberately `[]`, NOT `registry.roots()`, so a rejected sender is never handed the real persisted list either'), so the fix is self-documenting and internally consistent across all four handlers. `git diff` against the pre-fix version confirms this is the ONLY behavioral change to those two rejection branches. `npm run build` and `npm test` (845/845 tests, 127/127 files, run with TEMP/TMP redirected to a scratch dir to route around the known unrelated stray-`.orky`-under-OS-temp contamination) both pass with this change in place. No new dedicated regression test asserting the rejection payload's `roots` field specifically was added (the fix text's own suggested test), but the code fix itself is correct, complete, and introduces no new problem.

location: src/main/orky/orky-registry.ts:116-128 (removeRoot) vs :97-110 (addRoot) and :151-160 (resolveCanonical) + src/main/orky/validate-root.ts:24-27 (normalizeProjectRoot) — REQ-002, REQ-010 — this loopback's FINDING-DA-001 fix
claim: This loopback's FINDING-DA-001 fix added `resolveCanonical` (keyed by `normalizeProjectRoot`'s path.resolve()+win32-case-fold comparison key) to addRoot and trackPaneRoot, so a case/slash-divergent spelling of an ALREADY-TRACKED root collapses onto the existing canonical entry instead of creating a second, un-collapsed membership entry. removeRoot was NOT updated to use the same canonicalization: it only does a bare `resolve(input)` and an exact-string `this.persistedRoots.includes(normalized)` check. Consequence: a root that IS genuinely tracked — added via addRoot, or whose stored persistedRoots spelling was itself set to an earlier-opened pane's spelling by resolveCanonical's 'prefer the existing tracked spelling' behavior (addRoot stores `resolveCanonical(v.root)`, which can differ from the literal string the caller passed to addRoot) — silently FAILS to be removed when removeRoot is called with any case/slash-divergent but physically identical spelling of that same root: `persistedRoots.includes(normalized)` is false, so removeRoot takes REQ-010's defined 'root not in the list' branch and returns `{ok:true, roots:<unchanged>}` — reporting SUCCESS while the root remains tracked, its chokidar watcher keeps running, and it keeps appearing in the all-windows registry:status broadcast. This is concretely reachable on Windows (Termhalla's target platform): open a pane in a project (its spelling becomes the tracked canonical one), later addRoot the SAME project via a different case spelling (succeeds, but persistedRoots silently stores the PANE's spelling, not the caller's literal addRoot input, per resolveCanonical's contract) — a caller that later calls removeRoot with the spelling it originally typed into addRoot (rather than re-reading the addRoot response's `.root` field) gets a false `ok:true` and the project remains tracked/broadcast indefinitely. No test exercises this: the suite only ever feeds byte-identical canonical mkdtemp strings to both addRoot and removeRoot (the same structural gap FINDING-DA-002 already flagged for the merge/dedup path, now shown to also apply to removal). This breaks REQ-010's 'MUST remove the normalized root' guarantee and is inconsistent with the resolved-root identity model REQ-002/FINDING-DA-001 just established for the rest of the class.
fix: Route removeRoot's match through the SAME identity key addRoot/resolveCanonical use: resolve the input, fold it via `normalizeProjectRoot`, and find the `persistedRoots` entry whose `normalizeProjectRoot` matches that key — then remove THAT stored entry (not an exact-string `Array.includes`/`filter`). e.g. `const key = normalizeProjectRoot(normalized); const match = this.persistedRoots.find(r => normalizeProjectRoot(r) === key); if (!match) return {ok:true, roots:[...this.persistedRoots]}; this.persistedRoots = this.persistedRoots.filter(r => r !== match); ...; this.engine.removeConsumer(`persisted:${match}`)`. Add a test: addRoot one case/slash spelling, removeRoot a different case/slash spelling of the identical physical directory, assert the root is actually removed from `roots()` and the snapshot (mirrors the FINDING-DA-002 follow-up already requested for the analogous merge case).
resolution: Verified fixed in this loopback iteration. Re-read src/main/orky/orky-registry.ts:121-133: removeRoot now resolves the input via `this.resolveCanonical(resolve(input))` (wrapped in try/catch, still never-throws on a malformed input per REQ-010) BEFORE the `this.persistedRoots.includes(normalized)` check, instead of the prior bare `resolve(input)`. Traced resolveCanonical's behavior (lines 156-165, added by this same loopback's FINDING-DA-001 fix): it folds the input through `normalizeProjectRoot` (path.resolve + win32 case-fold) and, if an existing paneRoots or persistedRoots entry matches that folded key, returns THAT entry's exact stored spelling rather than the caller's input spelling. So for a case/slash-divergent removeRoot call naming an already-tracked root, `normalized` becomes literally equal to the stored persistedRoots string, and the subsequent exact-string `.includes()`/`.filter()`/`removeConsumer('persisted:'+normalized)` calls correctly match and tear down that exact tracked entry -- closing the false-`{ok:true}`-while-still-tracked gap the finding described. Confirmed the consumer-id symmetry holds: addRoot registers `persisted:${root}` where `root = this.resolveCanonical(v.root)` (same canonical form), so removeRoot's `removeConsumer('persisted:'+normalized)` now always targets the SAME key that was used to add it -- no orphaned watcher. Also confirmed the genuinely-absent-root and pane-only-root (not persisted) cases still correctly no-op with `{ok:true}` (resolveCanonical falls through to persistedRoots only after paneRoots, and a pane-only match still fails the persistedRoots.includes check as intended). No new dedicated regression test for the case/slash-divergent addRoot-then-removeRoot scenario was added (the fix text's own suggested test), but the logic fix itself is correct, complete, and consistent with the FINDING-DA-001 identity model it extends. `npm run build` and `npm test` (845/845 tests, 127/127 files, TEMP/TMP redirected to a scratch dir per the known unrelated stray-`.orky` contamination workaround) both pass with this change in place.

location: src/main/orky/orky-registry.ts:183-192 (resolveCanonical first-seen-wins) + :98 (trackPaneRoot canonical assignment) + :112 (addRoot canonical assignment) / REQ-007, REQ-006 -- this loopback FINDING-DA-001/FINDING-SEC-010 resolveCanonical diff
claim: resolveCanonical first-seen-tracked-spelling-wins rule makes the CANONICAL (displayed/persisted) root string, and therefore the codepoint-sort POSITION of that entry in the registry:status array, NON-DETERMINISTIC ACROSS RUNS when two CASE-divergent spellings of the SAME physical project arrive from different sources (two panes, or a pane + addRoot). resolveCanonical returns the first existing paneRoots/persistedRoots entry whose normalizeProjectRoot (path.resolve + win32 toLowerCase) key matches, else resolve(root). Because path.win32.resolve PRESERVES drive-letter/segment case (empirically confirmed: resolve(c:\dev\proj) stays c:\dev, resolve(C:\dev\proj) stays C:\dev), two spellings differing only in case do NOT collapse to one string on the no-match path -- only the internal comparison key folds case. So whichever spelling is observed FIRST becomes the canonical, and the arrival order of trackPaneRoot/addRoot is itself non-deterministic (each pane onPaneRoot fires only after its async findOrkyRoot resolves; fs scheduling decides which pane wins). Empirically confirmed the consequence is a genuine ARRAY REORDER, not just a cosmetic string swap: with a sibling root M:\foo, codepoint sort places C:\dev\proj BEFORE M:\foo (C=0x43 < M=0x4D) but c:\dev\proj AFTER it (c=0x63 > M=0x4D) -- so two runs with byte-identical logical inputs but different pane arrival timing emit registry:status snapshots whose entry ORDER differs. IMPORTANT SCOPE: this is NOT a REQ-007 contract violation and REQ-007 actual guarantee IS upheld end-to-end -- REQ-007 requires (a) codepoint sort, never localeCompare (buildRegistrySnapshot, frozen, does exactly this), and (b) that a RE-READ that does not change membership or status produces a byte-identical snapshot and never spuriously reorders (INTRA-run stability), which holds because the canonical is chosen once and stays fixed for the life of the run (verified: even after the first-seen pane closes, the second pane stored the CANONICAL not its own raw spelling, so no intra-run flip). The non-determinism is strictly CROSS-run / cross-event-timing, which REQ-007 was deliberately scoped NOT to cover. Functional impact is nil: dedup still yields exactly one entry (REQ-002) and status attaches correctly (post-FINDING-DA-001). The only effect is that a downstream aggregate consumer (F6 queue / F9 pane / F13 notifications) may display a case-divergent-source project under a different equivalent spelling and at a different list position on different app launches. Trigger is narrow (needs two CASE-divergent spellings -- slash-only divergence is already folded away by resolve() so it does NOT trigger this -- from divergent sources for one physical root; drive-letter case, e.g. c: vs C:, is the realistic vector on the Windows target platform). No existing finding records this: FINDING-DA-001 resolution confirmed dedup holds in BOTH orderings (one entry) but did not observe that the resulting canonical STRING and sort POSITION differ by ordering -- that determinism angle is distinct and previously unrecorded.
fix: Decide and record whether cross-run canonical determinism for case-divergent-source roots is required. Cheapest correct option if it is NOT required: add a one-line note under REQ-006/REQ-007 stating the canonical spelling of a project observed via two case-divergent spellings is the first-observed one (intra-run stable, cross-run timing-dependent) and that this is an accepted consequence of case-preserving the stored value (the frozen tests/main/validate-root.test.ts + orky-registry-service.test.ts assert byte-for-byte mixed-case mkdtemp paths, so case-folding the STORED value is not an option). If cross-run determinism IS wanted, the only mechanisms are (a) case-fold the stored/displayed value -- blocked by those frozen tests -- or (b) a deterministic tie-break (e.g. keep the codepoint-minimal spelling among all divergent spellings seen for a root), but note (b) requires re-canonicalizing an already-committed entry when a smaller spelling later arrives, which would introduce exactly the INTRA-run reorder REQ-007 forbids -- i.e. cross-run determinism and REQ-007 intra-run stability are in genuine tension here, so the honest resolution is likely to document (a-note), not to change the code. Do NOT case-fold the stored value.
